skill.name: researching-with-deepwiki
skill.description: Research remote GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket repositories with the DeepWiki MCP server. Use it to explore unfamiliar codebases, understand architecture, and ask source-code questions.
skill.summary: Research remote repositories with DeepWiki MCP for architecture, structure, and implementation questions.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static external-command findings are false positives from Markdown fences, inline examples, and quoted prompts. The confirmed risk is legitimate third-party MCP network use, including optional private-repository bearer-token configuration. No prompt injection, malicious command execution, or credential exfiltration code was found.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Service Handles Repository Queries
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to configure DeepWiki HTTP MCP endpoints and send repository questions to that service. This is expected behavior for the skill, but users should treat repository names, prompts, and private-repository content as data shared with a third party.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The DeepWiki HTTP endpoint is explicitly documented and is central to the skill. The risk is data exposure to a third-party service, not hidden malware.

medium_findings.title: Private Repository Token Configuration Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows an Authorization bearer-token header for private repositories. This is a placeholder rather than a hardcoded secret, but users may expose sensitive repository data if they configure real tokens without understanding the endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token line is a clear placeholder and not a leaked credential. The security concern is operational handling of real private-repository credentials.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences, quoted prompts, tables, or inline verification text. They do not execute commands when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation syntax and natural-language examples. No shell interpreter, Ruby runtime, eval path, or command invocation is present in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Verification Script Reads Local Settings
low_findings.description: The verifier reads common Claude settings files to check whether a DeepWiki MCP server is configured. It does not transmit or print credentials, but local configuration reads should remain limited and transparent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly opens local settings JSON files and inspects MCP server entries. No network call or outbound data path appears in the script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party MCP Endpoint With Optional Authorization Header
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines a remote HTTP MCP endpoint with optional private-repository authorization. This pattern is legitimate here, but it warrants user warning because prompts and repository access may leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented URL and Authorization header are direct evidence. No evidence shows malicious intent or hidden exfiltration beyond the intended external service.

content.value_statement: Large repositories are hard to understand from file browsing alone. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use DeepWiki for focused repository research.
actual_capabilities: Configures use of the public DeepWiki MCP endpoint for remote repository analysis.
Documents a Devin.ai endpoint option for private repository research with authentication.
Guides architecture, directory structure, and feature implementation questions.
Provides example prompts for comparing implementations across repositories.
Explains when to use local file tools instead of DeepWiki.
limitations: It depends on an external DeepWiki MCP service being configured.
Private repositories require a separate Devin.ai API key.
Cached analysis may not include the newest commits.
It is not intended for local files or library API reference lookup.

use_cases.title: Understand a new framework
use_cases.description: Ask targeted questions about architecture, modules, and core patterns in an unfamiliar open-source project.

use_cases.title: Prepare a contribution
use_cases.description: Study repository structure, testing patterns, and contribution workflows before opening a pull request.

use_cases.title: Compare design choices
use_cases.description: Compare how two repositories solve caching, routing, authentication, or middleware design problems.

prompt_templates.title: Repository overview
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a first pass through a repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the architecture of github.com/{owner}/{repo}. Summarize the main modules and responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Directory explanation
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused request for one part of a project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the {path} directory in github.com/{owner}/{repo}. Include key files and how they work together.

prompt_templates.title: Feature investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: How is {feature} implemented in github.com/{owner}/{repo}? Identify the main components and data flow.

prompt_templates.title: Cross-repository comparison
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for design analysis across projects.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare how github.com/{owner1}/{repo1} and github.com/{owner2}/{repo2} handle {feature}. Highlight tradeoffs.

output: A concise architecture summary with core modules, request flow, and main extension points.
A map of routing, dependency injection, middleware, and response handling responsibilities.
Follow-up questions for deeper analysis of selected subsystems.

output_examples.input: How does authentication work in a selected repository?
output: A feature-focused explanation of authentication entry points and session handling.
Relevant directories or modules to inspect next.
Warnings about assumptions when cached analysis may be outdated.

output_examples.input: Compare cache invalidation across two repositories.
output: A side-by-side summary of each design approach.
Tradeoffs for consistency, performance, complexity, and developer ergonomics.
Practical lessons that may apply to a new project.

best_practices: Ask specific architecture or feature questions instead of broad repository summaries.
Include repository URLs and paths when you already know the area of interest.
Use local tools for code already available on disk.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential repository content to DeepWiki without approval.
Do not use it as a replacement for official library API documentation.
Do not assume cached analysis reflects commits made today.

faq.question: What repositories can this skill research?
faq.answer: It is designed for GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket repositories that DeepWiki can access.

faq.question: Does it work with private repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, but private repositories require the Devin.ai endpoint and a valid bearer token.

faq.question: Does this skill execute repository code?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides prompts and setup guidance for DeepWiki-based research.

faq.question: Is repository data sent to an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. DeepWiki is an external MCP service, so avoid unauthorized confidential data sharing.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it for local files, quick exact text searches, or official API reference lookup.

faq.question: How can I verify the setup?
faq.answer: Run the included verifier to check whether a DeepWiki MCP server is configured.
